data.title: SPIN: The Overlooked 8% Yielding Covered Call ETF Income Investors Are Sleeping On
data.description:
Introduction

With the vast amount of covered call funds that have hit the market in recent years, it's never been easier for investors to create a steady stream of income.

With inflation likely to remain above historical levels for the near to medium term, I believe we will see more funds hit the market in the coming years.

With the vast selection, I can see why it's easy for investors to overlook State Street's (STT) US Equity Premium Income ETF (SPIN). Despite this, SPIN has been quietly performing well and has even outperformed some popular peers since its inception.

In this article, I discuss SPIN's strategy, performance, and why income-focused investors should consider this 8% yielding covered call ETF for their portfolio.

The Need for Income

When I first joined the military at 18 years old, I quickly learned that the need for income was important. This is because I would see so many service members retire and have to go directly back to work.

Part of this was due to their lack of financial literacy and planning. Another was due to inflation, something I wasn't tracking at that age. But we all know that items get more expensive over time.

Insurance, homes, automobiles, etc. Over time, these items get more expensive, thus creating a need for income. Whether this be through dividends or other measures.

And in 2026 and beyond, I think this is more important than ever. The days of a household relying on one source of income are likely long gone. While many Americans can make a decent salary, many still struggle with small items like having an emergency savings.

According to a study, more than 52% of Americans can't cover their monthly expenses. This is why I believe investors should invest for wealth and income and not solely focus on one.

My personal belief is that every investor should have at least two portfolios. One primarily focused on income, and one either a mix of income & high-growth stocks or a portfolio solely focused on growth.

Whether this be through a taxable account or a tax-advantaged one like a Roth IRA. The chart below shows the inflows into covered call ETFs since 2015. You can see starting in 2022 is when inflows really spiked, likely due to inflation.

I don't believe this is a coincidence since the Federal Reserve started raising interest rates in 2022 and hiked them their fastest in history. And although base rates have come down, items are still overly expensive.

But quality dividend-paying stocks or covered call ETFs could be used to alleviate the pain. And here's why I believe SPIN is one income-focused fund investors should consider.

Previous Thesis

I last covered SPIN back in September in an article along with another covered call fund, assigning it a buy rating.

Due to its yield above 8% and ability to potentially preserve investor capital, SPIN was an attractive option for investors looking for a steady stream of income.

Despite their short track record, SPIN's low expense ratio, reputable asset manager in State Street, and solid performance made them a viable option for income seekers.

Strategy

Like many covered call ETFs, SPIN employs a similar strategy to peers like JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) and Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX) in that it holds actual U.S. equities instead of using a synthetic strategy.

All three use the S&P (SP500) as a benchmark and write options on its underlying holdings to generate additional income. For SPIN, the fund uses an actively managed strategy.

Their investment objective is to provide investors with current income with the potential to see long-term capital appreciation.

Me, personally, I prefer funds that hold actual equities, as I believe they are able to capture more upside from their underlying holdings. Funds like the TappAlpha SPY Growth & Daily Income ETF (TSPY) hold the actual index instead of individual holdings.

Currently, behemoths like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL), and Apple Inc. (AAPL) are the ETF's largest holdings, as Technology stocks, like those in the State Street® Technology Select Sector SPDR® (XLK) make up the largest sector.

Technology accounts for approximately 34% of the portfolio. Mega-cap financial stocks like Visa Inc. (V) and JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) make up the Financial sector — companies like those found in the State Street® Financial Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLF) — while one of my personal favorite dividend growth stocks, T-Mobile US, Inc. (TMUS), is also in the portfolio.

Communication Services, with stocks like those in the State Street® Communication Services Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLC), makes up the ETF's third-largest sector. Both Financials & Communications Services account for 13.82% and 11.29% of SPIN's portfolio, respectively.

SPIN holds U.S. large- and mid-cap stocks and looks to hold companies with a market cap north of $2 billion. Management then writes call options, usually month to month, but on those with an expiration date of less than one year.

They also can employ FLEX options, which allows them to customize things like strike price and expiration dates. Management typically selects shorter date options with a strike price close to the current price of the underlying holding.

They also use ladder options, meaning they deploy multiple call options to reduce the risk of single options. This also creates more opportunities to generate income and harvest higher premiums during higher periods of market volatility.

Management will also close options once they're close to their expiration dates, which is known as rolling the options contract. This is when you close the current contract and start a new one with a different date and strike price. Known as rolling up and out.

Performance

When it comes to how SPIN has performed, the ETF has been no slouch. In comparison to some heavy hitters like GPIX, JEPI, and NEOS' S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI), the former has outperformed all but GPIX in price returns.

While higher-yielding funds typically have higher expense ratios closer to 1%, GS has some of the lowest cost structures at 0.29%. Meaning you'd only pay $29 for every $10,000 invested. SPIN beats this with an expense ratio of just 0.25%, the lowest I've personally witnessed. So, despite a short track record, SPIN outperformed both JEPI & SPYI in price returns in the past year.

In total returns, SPYI bested the group, up 16.94% to 16.72% for both SPIN & GPIX. This is slightly higher than the S&P's 16.39% for 2025.

Over the past 6 months, however, SPIN beat the entire peer group and doubled JEPI's total returns in the process.

Since September 2024, SPIN has lagged both GPIX & SPYI but again doubled JEPI, up 22.19%. Again, I believe NEOS and Goldman Sachs are two of the top asset managers today for covered call funds. And their performances speak to this.

The fact that SPIN has managed to not only outperform but also perform similarly also speaks to its quality despite being overshadowed by larger peers. Which should be of no surprise since STT owns the first U.S. ETF, the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

And although 2026 could see some volatility due to geopolitical uncertainty, I remain bullish on the index.

And believe we will potentially see another double-digit performance. As a result of the market likely seeing at least returns in the double digits, SPIN should see solid total returns in the 10% to 12% range in 2026.

SPIN's performance can be attributed to their solid NAV growth, something a lot of covered call funds fail to see. Many see NAV erosion, which often leads to deteriorating distributions as well as price underperformance.

But the chart below shows SPIN's NAV has grown nicely since inception.

Distributions and Taxes

Like many covered call funds, SPIN's distributions are a mix of return of capital and net investment income.

Because of this, most would have their taxes deferred, making them advantageous to hold inside a taxable account. Taxes will be deferred until the investor sells or their cost basis reaches zero.

For example, each year the return of capital portion of your distributions lowers your cost basis, carrying your cost basis to subsequent years. Once you sell, your capital gains will be calculated according to your adjusted cost basis.

If you decide to sell before your cost basis reaches zero, you would only be taxed on the capital gain. If you sell after it reaches zero, any future ROC will be taxed at the long-term capital gains rate. This is important to note for when you decide to sell this position.

SPIN also has a lower yield at 8.13%, which also makes them less appealing since peers typically have yields in the double digits. They also pay monthly. And for 2025, the fund paid an average distribution of $0.22.

Because SPIN's distributions also consist of NII, investors may be subject to different taxes. Net investment income is taxed differently depending on your filing status and threshold.

Here are two examples.

Risks and Conclusion

A major risk for SPIN is their smaller size in comparison to peers. SPYI has $7 billion in AUM, while GPIX has close to $3 billion. Despite their solid performance, SPIN only has $56.32 million in AUM, making them less appealing for some.

Something else investors should be aware of is SPIN's distributions have higher variations in comparison to SPYI and GPIX. This could also be a turn-off for some collecting passive income in retirement.

And lastly, if the market experiences a steep crash or correction, this could drag on the fund's NAV, leading to price erosion temporarily.

However, due to their call options strategy that caps their upside, funds like SPIN experience the full impact of market drawdowns. This can also impact total returns.

But due to SPIN's conservative yield, this allows the fund to retain capital instead of paying out most of their income, which also negatively impacts NAV.

On the flip side, distributions would likely increase in market volatility, similar to the months following the Liberation Day sell-off in April of last year.

Furthermore, I remain bullish on the S&P in 2026 due to AI enthusiasm and believe we will see another strong year, up double digits. As a result of this and SPIN's ability to capture the index's upside, I continue to rate the fund a buy.

data.title: Sezzle: Volatility Provides Opportunity In BNPL
data.description:
Sezzle Inc. (SEZL) has experienced a multi-year tailwind from the strong trends across the BNPL (buy now pay later) market. During the COVID period of accelerated spending trends, there were several BNPL players that came to market. At the time, there were high levels of skepticism regarding the durability of these business models. However, over the past few years and buoyed by strong consumer spending, Sezzle has been one of the few BNPL companies that have experienced high levels of success.

Sezzle is a financial technology company that provides BNPL loans to consumers. This gives consumers the ability to split up an expense into installment payments, typically four interest-free installments over a short duration. Sezzle partners with online and in-store retailers, giving consumers increased flexibility. Sezzle has expanded more into subscription services and on-demand financial products (ex: WebBank), diversifying its revenue stream beyond the pure transaction-fee model.

2025 was another roller-coaster year for Sezzle. As seen in the chart above, the volatile swings in stock price have been correlated with the company's earnings reports. The big spike in May 2025 was driven by a very strong Q1 report, which saw revenue grow 120% yoy and material margin improvement. In fact, the company raised the full-year guidance after just one quarter.

However, after a period of bullish momentum that saw shares nearly 4x in just a few months, sentiment became too hot. The Q2 report in August was challenging and caused a near 40% pullback in shares over the following days. High expectations combined with no guidance raise (implied deceleration for the remainder of the year) was a double-edged sword.

A more balanced Q3 report was still met with a negative stock move as sentiment adjusted lower. However, performance over the past few months has been strong, with renewed momentum and interest across BNPL. The consumer remains healthy, and with more optimism across the space, and in particular with Sezzle's competitive positioning, has left many investors encouraged.

My argument from here is quite simple. The business momentum is strong, and the company's financial profile is impressive. Innovation across its product suite is encouraging, and the ramping profitability is helping support a premium valuation. The biggest pushback I have is around the growing bullish momentum, which may cause expectations for the company's upcoming full-year guidance to rise. If Sezzle is not able to produce guidance that is above the "whisper" number, the stock may be prone to another correction.

For now, I am bullish, but would be somewhat hesitant to build out a full position into earnings. With more clarity coming in just a few weeks, fortune may favor the patient.

Bullish Trends and Business Momentum

The company's latest Q3 earnings report in November was more balanced relative to the prior two quarters of volatility. But balanced does not mean bad. In fact, trends were still strong, and profitability improved.

As seen in the chart above, revenue grew 67% yoy, which was a deceleration relative to the 76% yoy growth in Q2, but this was as expected. In my opinion, the two key positives during the quarter were adjusted EBITDA margins expanding ~150 bps and revenue as a percentage of GMV expanding to 11.2%, up from 10.6% in the year-ago quarter. With Sezzle being successful at capturing more of the economics of GMV, this has direct positive impacts to profitability.

When it comes to guidance, there were minimal changes, with the biggest delta coming from changes to discrete tax items. Importantly, Sezzle provided a 2026 EPS baseline target of $4.35, which is a good starting point when looking at valuation -- more on that below.

One aspect of Sezzle's changing business model is its current transition to focus more on subscription revenue. The company has gone back and forth over the years, pivoting their focus between on-demand revenue and subscription revenue. As management noted below, they are more positive on the prospects of subscription revenue.

On-demand has clearly positioned us to be more aggressive with enterprise merchants. However, it didn't deliver like we hoped on conversions at the point of sale or over to subscription. Further, the profit profile for on-demand is less than our premium and anywhere subscription products. We still believe on-demand is a great tool, and it's a great tool to have in our tool belt, but we have adjusted how we go to market with it.

It was probably right around the middle of the quarter [when on-demand was de-emphasized]. At that point, we felt like we had enough data based on what we had been seeing on conversion at point of sale, conversion into subscription. And the bridge just wasn't as strong as we were originally envisioning. Conversions, I think, were slightly better into on-demand at point of sale than they are into subscription, but just not enough to make the payout worthwhile. And so when we started to analyze the lifetime values of the customers, the conversion rates, we really started to realize that on-demand is probably just a better tool around the fringes and at least in the direct-to-consumer portion of our business.

However, it should be noted that going through any type of business model transition is not easy. Internally, this can be challenging from a sales and marketing perspective. And from a customer viewpoint, this could cause some friction at the point of sale, when purchasing decisions are made. Success here is not guaranteed and will be something investors will be closely watching.

As mentioned above, Subscription is a more profitable revenue bucket, which itself is a great reason to make this transition. Sezzle did see strong early success from its on-demand product, but the majority of customers come in the form of subscribers, with the company seeing 10x more orders from subscribers vs. non-subscribers.

Longer-term, I believe this business model transition will be positive, but there could be some challenges along the way. As the company de-emphasizes its on-demand product, there could be some direct negative impacts on revenue, which could distort some of the profitability.

Over the past week, President Trump has discussed the potential 1-year 10% credit card interest rate cap for financial institutions. While the ultimate outcome is uncertain, I believe this could improve the value proposition for BNPL players.

Strong opposition is certain from Wall Street in addition to the credit card companies, which donated heavily to his 2024 campaign and have supported Trump’s second-term agenda. Banks are making the argument that such a plan would most hurt poor people, at a time of economic concern, by curtailing or eliminating credit lines, driving them to high-cost alternatives like payday loans or pawnshops.

The average credit card interest rate is hovering around 24%, and capping this would have direct negative impacts for financial institutions lending to riskier credit profiles. However, these consumers will likely still maintain their spending habits; they will just need to find alternative solutions, such as BNPL.

This news is still developing, and there is no clear outcome; however, the market has already punished the entire financial institution complex given the headline risk.

Valuation

Given the competitive nature of the BNPL industry and the varying growth/profit metrics, there is a wide range of valuations. As expected, the more established, faster growth companies carry premium multiples, while the more speculative plays trade at a discount.

Given the success Sezzle has experienced over the past year, and with the renewed investor optimism about the business, the company currently trades at 17.4x forward EPS.

Sezzle has a strong revenue growth profile, and the transition back to subscription revenue could end up benefiting margins over the long term. The company provided a 2026 EPS target of $4.35, which, absent any material one-time headwinds, appears achievable.

Assuming another strong year of growth combined with margin expansion, there is a path for 2027 EPS to reach $5.25+. I believe this could start to be the baseline of expectations for the stock. This would mean the stock is currently trading at ~14.5x 2027 EPS, which screens attractive given the growth potential here.

With earnings estimates biased higher over the next year, the valuation multiple does not need to move for the stock to keep working.

Risks

In my opinion, the biggest risk to my bull thesis is competition. Although the number of BNPL companies has consolidated over the years, competition is still high. The leading player in the market is Affirm, though several other players, such as Afterpay, Klarna, PayPal, and others, have amassed meaningful market share. With high competition often comes some level of price wars. If Sezzle is not able to innovate its credit solutions that appeal to consumers, competition may take market share.

Consumer spending is another area of risk. If consumer confidence and/or spending were to deteriorate, this would not only result in lower transaction volumes, but also potentially higher credit loss exposure.

Additionally, there are some risks around regulation. Although the market still operates rather freely today, new legislation could prohibit growth. For example, the Trump Administration is looking to cap credit card interest rates at 10%. The indirect impacts on BNPL are yet to be seen, but if legislation were to directly impact the BNPL lending environment, this would be a clear negative.

Final Thoughts

I believe BNPL has carved out a niche within broader consumer spending patterns, and one that is likely to stay long-term. Sure, the market is highly fragmented and competitive, but Sezzle has proven it has some level of staying power. The company has a strong growth story, improving margins, and product innovation that is appealing to consumers.

Although the stock is up over 40% since its November lows, there is still another 2-3x potential from here just to recapture its all-time highs. Is this a guarantee? Of course not. But I believe Sezzle is well positioned to maintain the bullish momentum for 2026.

data.title: Sales Of Existing Homes In 2025 Drop To Lowest Since 1995, Sellers Massively Yank Listings Off The Market, Waiting For Spring
data.description:
Single-family home sales edged up in 2025, condo sales dropped further.

Annual sales of all types of existing homes declined by 0.2% in 2025 from the prior year, the fourth year in a row of declines, to 4.06 million homes, the lowest since 1995, with single-family home sales ticking up a hair and condo and co-op sales dropping further.

Monthly sales that closed in December rose by 1.4% year-over-year, to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 4.35 million homes, the best December since 2021, but still down by 29% from December 2021, according to the National Association of Realtors today.

For the past three years, home sales have run essentially at the same collapsed pace, down by 34% from the pandemic high of 2021 and down by 43% from the all-time high in 2005 (historical data via YCharts).

Single-family home sales in 2025 ticked up by 0.5% to 3.69 million homes. But that was still down by 32% from the pandemic high in 2021 and down by 40% from the all-time high in 2005.

Monthly sales that closed in December rose by 1.8% year-over-year to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 3.95 million homes, the best December since 2021, but down by 27% from December 2021.

Condo and Co-op sales in 2025 fell by 4.6% to 373,000, the lowest in the data by NAR going back to 2009, down by 32% from the pandemic high in 2021, and down by 40% from the all-time high in 2021.

But the condo bubble of 2021, when sales exploded to 707,000 and prices exploded in many markets, is now sticking out like a sore thumb. Since then, condo prices have been careening down in many markets and in some markets are down by 30% and more from their peaks in prior years.

Monthly condo sales that closed in December fell by 2.4% year-over-year to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 400,000 condos, along with December 2023 the worst December on record, and down by 41% from December 2021.

Yanking their listings off the market over the holidays

Supply of homes for sale always plunges in December as sellers yank their listings off the market over the holidays, only to relist them in the spring.

In November, supply of all homes combined had been the highest for any November in many years. But in December, sellers ran out of patience, and the yanking-fest was bigger than in some prior Decembers, especially with condos.

Supply of single-family homes fell to 3.3 months in December (red line with big squares in the chart below), a steep drop from November (4.0 months), which had been the highest for any November since 2018.

But even the sharply lower supply in December was still the highest for any December since 2018.

In terms of condos, sellers yanked their condos off the market at a stunning pace over the holidays, amid little demand and big haircuts on prices in many markets – to relist them in the spring.

Only 32,000 condos were sold in December, but inventory plunged by 79,000 condos, or by 42.5%, from the prior month. That’s the force of delisting over the holidays.

Supply — which is inventory divided by the sales rate — plunged to 3.2 months from 5.6 months in November, which had been the highest for any November since 2011 during the housing bust.

The national median price of single-family homes fell by 1.3% in December from November along seasonal patterns, to $409,500, just 0.2% above December 2024 ($408,500).

This national median price of single-family homes had exploded by 47% from June 2020 through June 2025, most of it during the two years from mid-2020 to mid-2022.

January or February usually marks the seasonal low each year, and June the seasonal high. The index is not seasonally adjusted. The seasonal zigzag is a result of shifts in the mix of what is on the market and sells, which shifts the median price up or down.

But price dynamics differ dramatically from market to market, and the national median price is meaningless for specific markets. In some markets, home prices have fallen substantially, including by 24% in the Austin-Round Rock-San Marcos metro, the biggest drop in our lineup of 33 large and expensive housing markets. Conversely, the Milwaukee-Waukesha metro is the market with the biggest year-over-year gain (+4.1%) and record prices.

The national median price of condos and co-ops rose by 1.3% in December from November, on dismal sales volume that shifted the mix of what sold. Year-over-year, the median price was up by 1.5%.

This national median price of condos and co-ops had exploded by 43% from mid-2020 through mid-2025, most of it during the two years of mid-2020 to mid-2022.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The One Retirement Portfolio I'd Trust With My Entire Passive Income
data.description:
If I were to retire tomorrow, my primary concern would not be beating the S&P 500 (SPY). Instead, it would be generating a reliable, inflation-resistant income stream that would also be as tax-efficient as possible. Thus, I would be looking to invest in securities that provide the highest and most dependable income streams over the long term and invest in them in such a way that my tax burden would be minimized so that I would get the best possible after-tax passive income stream that should last me through all sorts of economic and market environments.

I thus would want to invest in a portfolio that was inflation- and recession-resistant, because these are the two environments in which passive income streams often fall behind the most, with dividend cuts striking during recessions and inflation eroding purchasing power. Additionally, the tax efficiency component is important because it puts me in the most advantageous position possible to generate attractive passive income over the long term. This includes attacking the tax hit from two different directions.

First of all, minimize exposure to C-corporations that get hit with hefty taxation and are exposed to the potential for corporate income tax rising with shifting political winds. Additionally, approach pass-through entities, which evade the corporate income tax, in such a way that their distributions are as tax-sheltered as possible. Thus, you can ultimately invest in a portfolio of attractive income generators without having to pay much at all in the way of taxes, thereby giving yourself a significant boost to your effective income yield and putting yourself on the path to long-term alpha without even having to make any intelligent investing moves.

In particular, I like to invest in limited partnerships (AMLP), real estate investment trusts (VNQ), and business development companies (BIZD) because these are three forms of publicly traded pass-through entities that tend to pay out very high yields and do not get hit with corporate income taxes. Note that this article is not tax advice, and there may be securities mentioned that issue a K-1 tax form, in particular among LPs. Therefore, you need to be sure to do your own tax due diligence before investing in any of these securities or following any of the strategies mentioned in this article, because it may not be suitable for all investors’ personal circumstances.

What I Would Hold in Taxable Accounts

In my taxable account, I like to hold MLPs because they are most tax-efficient in a taxable account. This is because their distributions are generally classified as return of capital, which means you do not pay any taxes on them until you sell them, unless your cost basis eventually goes to zero. However, you can reinvest a portion of your distributions into buying more units of these businesses, which can help rebuild some of your cost basis, thereby lengthening the time period until you have to pay taxes on distributions.

Additionally, if you hold them until you pass away, their cost basis gets reset to the price at which they are at when they get passed on, thereby making all the passive income you’ve generated from them over the years effectively tax-free. Alternatively, you can donate these units to a nonprofit and not only get your passive income over the years tax-free, but you also get a nice tax write-off up to the value of the units at the time they are donated.

Tax considerations aside, MLPs are also very attractive income vehicles because they generate quite stable cash flows, given that most of them have very low exposure to commodity price volatility and instead generate the vast majority of their cash flows from long-duration contracts, with revenues being based on fees and volumes instead. Moreover, these businesses tend to have well-diversified business models by geographic region and commodity type, which further enhances their cash flow stability.

They also tend to be inflation-resistant due to the inflation-linked escalators on their contracted assets, and the energy sector (XLE) in general tends to benefit from inflation over time. Moreover, blue-chip MLPs like Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Energy Transfer (ET), and MPLX (MPLX) offer yields in the high single digits, which at the moment is around 7–8%, which are covered comfortably by distributable cash flow. Additionally, they’re backed by investment-grade credit ratings with leverage ratios on the low end of the target ranges for these companies, making them very sound cash flow machines.

Not only that, but they are also growing their distributions at rates that meet or beat inflation over time. Therefore, between the strong balance sheets, highly contracted cash flows, and the inflation protections, these are excellent stagflation-resistant income vehicles that also happen to be very tax-efficient in a taxable account. Note that if you put them in a retirement account, many times they generate UBTI if you cross certain thresholds. Therefore, I would hold them exclusively in a taxable account.

If you felt the need to hold them in an IRA or a 401k, look into an ETF like AMLP or the Global X MLP ETF (MLPA) or a 1099-issuing C-corporation like Enbridge (ENB), though in each of these cases, the holdings would likely have to pay corporate income tax at some point. So that is a major negative.

What I Would Hold in Roth Accounts

Meanwhile, in my Roth accounts, I would want to hold BDCs, REITs, and Brookfield (BAM)(BN) LPs like Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP) and Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP). This is because all of these businesses are suitable for holding in a Roth in that they do not generate UBTI, and they are also pass-through entities. In a taxable account, BDC and REIT distributions are taxable, and Brookfield Infrastructure Partners and Brookfield Renewable Partners often generate taxable income as well, which can raise your tax bill when you file the K-1 that BIP and BEP generate. When held in a Roth, though, all that gets sheltered, and you owe no taxes on their future gains.

BDCs are also very attractive entities for a retirement income portfolio because they tend to generate very high yields, with the likes of Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC), Main Street Capital (MAIN), Capital Southwest Corporation (CSWC), Hercules Capital (HTGC), Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), and others generating yields in the range of 7–11% per year while backing those yields with portfolios that primarily consist of senior secured loans, asset-backed loans, and other investments in middle-market businesses.

Moreover, their portfolios are very well diversified and are managed by some of the leading asset management teams in the world, who can access proprietary deal flow, have strong underwriting track records, and actively manage the businesses to help reduce losses in the event that things go poorly. You combine all that with the sky-high yield that these BDCs offer, and these become an attractive component of an income portfolio.

Not only that, but BDCs also generally benefit from rising short-term interest rates since they invest primarily in floating-rate loans that are indexed to short-term interest rates. Therefore, in an environment where the Fed is hiking rates and the economy remains on at least a decent footing, BDCs tend to outperform, as they did from 2022 to 2024, when the Fed began pivoting toward rate cuts.

Meanwhile, REITs are also attractive to hold in a Roth because their dividends are often at least partially classified as ordinary income, though some of them are also often classified as return of capital or have some other tax benefits. Moreover, they’re also attractive income generators because they generally have long-duration leases, tend to be defensive in nature due to the critical nature of real estate to many businesses or individuals who use them for shelter, and also tend to have contractual rent escalators, either CPI-linked or fixed.

Additionally, if you invest in triple-net lease REITs like W. P. Carey (WPC), Realty Income (O), and NNN REIT (NNN), they also tend to be highly recession-resistant. Multifamily REITs like Camden Property Trust (CPT), Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA), and AvalonBay (AVB) also tend to be at least somewhat recession-resistant due to their critical nature. These types of REITs both tend to have consistent dividend growth track records as well, due to their defensive nature, which makes them good, dependable dividend payers in a retiree’s portfolio.

Meanwhile, they also tend to deliver mid-single-digit yields, which are pretty attractive, especially when considering the defensive nature. However, because they have long-duration leases and are not quite as inflation-resistant as midstream MLPs, they tend to perform best when interest rates are falling and perform worst when interest rates are rising. Therefore, they provide a nice counterbalance to BDCs since they tend to perform better in different environments.

This explains why REITs significantly lagged BDCs between 2022 and mid-2024.

Meanwhile, Brookfield Infrastructure Partners and Brookfield Renewable Partners also tend to outperform when interest rates are falling since they also have long-duration, bond-like cash flow structures. However, they do have highly inflation-linked contracted cash flows that make them pretty good stagflation investments while also providing attractive diversification into other areas of infrastructure, including renewable power, hydropower, nuclear energy, transportation infrastructure, and data centers, while paying out attractive distribution yields of between 5–6%, backed by BBB+ credit ratings and high single-digit to low double-digit annualized growth rates.

How These Assets Balance Each Other Across Market Cycles

While this portfolio will likely experience a lot of volatility in the event of a market crash, outside of that, it should be well diversified because these different types of securities complement each other nicely, with broad diversification and different performances in rising interest rate and falling interest rate environments. Additionally, all of these asset types are quite defensive in nature, meaning that the income stream should be fairly resilient to recessions while also generating a weighted average yield in the high single digits, thereby making them a great fit for a retiree looking to retire on passive income from investments.

Of course, you can further diversify this portfolio by investing in traditional blue-chip dividend growth C-corporations through individual stock picks or even through a diversified ETF like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). You can also give yourself exposure to precious metals (GLD) income through either dividend-paying gold mining stocks (GDX) or, even better, gold and silver bullion leases, which tend to have a higher yield and lower volatility and operating risk than gold miners do.

Moreover, you can add in other income funds like PIMCO’s actively managed closed-end funds (PDI)(PDO) that pay out high yields, and you can also even invest a small amount into a covered-call, mega-cap tech-heavy ETF like the NEOs NASDAQ High Income ETF (QQQI) or the JPMorgan NASDAQ Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), among others.

Ultimately, however, I think the securities discussed in this article create a great backbone for a tax-efficient, relatively defensive, and sustainable high-yield portfolio. As such, they are my leading go-to sectors for positions that I hold in my high-yielding, low-beta, and outperforming portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: TSMC, Lam Research Spark Early Market Rally
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Biggest stock movers Thursday: DELL, LRCX, TSM and more. (00:13) Verizon (VZ) restores service after outage, will give credits to affected customers. (01:28) Bilt introduces three new cards with 10% introductory APR. (02:13)

This is an abridged transcript.

The biggest movers list this morning includes names like Dell Technologies, Nokia, Lam Research and Taiwan Semiconductor. Let’s look at the latter two.

Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) +5% - Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), a major supplier to companies including Nvidia (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL), reported a net profit of NT$506B (US$16B) for the October-December quarter, a 35% surge from a year earlier, better than analysts’ estimates. TSMC also said it plans to boost its capital expenditure budget to $52B-$56B for 2026, up from about $40B last year.

Lam Research (LRCX) +6% - Stifel is forecasting wafer fabrication equipment spending to increase by 10% to 15% in calendar year 2026, which should benefit Teradyne (TER), Lam Research (LRCX) and KLA (KLAC). This equates to a $10B to $15B year-over-year increase, which will be led by advanced foundry/logic and DRAM. The financial firm had expected WFE growth of 7% to 8% in a previous forecast. Teradyne (TER) is up 4% and KLA (KLAC) is up 5% in premarket action.

Verizon (VZ) said that it has restored its network services following a major nationwide outage on Wednesday that affected thousands of customers. Over 171,000 reports were logged on DownDetector, primarily involving loss of mobile signal.

Verizon (VZ) apologized for the inconvenience and reiterated plans to issue account credits directly to impacted customers, with details to follow. No specific cause for the interruption was disclosed.

"Today, we let many of our customers down and for that, we are truly sorry. They expect more from us. We are working non-stop and making progress. Our teams will continue to work through the night until service is restored for all impacted customers. We will make this right - for any customer affected, we will provide account credits and share updates soon," the company said.

Other carriers like AT&T (T) and T-Mobile (TMUS) also reported disruptions, though at significantly lower levels.

Bilt is introducing three new credit cards, under the Bilt Card 2.0 name, that offer a 10% introductory APR for 12 months across all three products. That comes less than a week after President Trump said he plans to place a 10% cap on credit card interest rates for a year.

Bilt originally started as a card that provided rewards to apartment renters. "Today, that promise extends to mortgage payments," the company said. The Bilt 2.0 card is offered in partnership with Cardless, Fidem Financial, and Column N.A.

"Bilt Card 2.0 offers rewards on your rent, rewards on your mortgage, and earns you the most valuable points in the market," said Ankur Jain, founder and CEO of Bilt.

The Bilt Palladium Card, with an annual fee of $495, offers 2x points on everyday spending, 4% back in BiltCash on everyday purchases, and a limited-time 50,000 sign-up bonus. The Bilt Obsidian Card, with a $95 annual fee, offers 3x points on dining and groceries, 2x on travel, 1x on everyday spending, and 4% back in Bilt Cash on everyday spending.

Bilt Blue Card, with no annual fee, offers 1x points on everyday spending and 4% back in Bilt Cash on everyday spending, the company said.

Palladium cardholders get $300 in Bilt Cash on opening an account, Obsidian holders get $200, and Bilt Blue holders get $100 in Bilt Cash.

American Express (AXP), Bank of America (BAC), Citigroup (C), Capital One Financial (COF), Discover Financial (DFS), JPMorgan (JPM), and Synchrony Financial (SYF) are all due to update on credit card net charge-offs and delinquency rates for December.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.5% and the DAX is up 0.3%.

data.title: Zumiez: How Private Labels Are Reshaping Its Margins
data.description:
Q3 wasn't pretty for Zumiez (ZUMZ) overseas, but back home in the U.S., margins came in a lot stronger than I had penciled in, delivering a second consecutive double-beat.

Once you look at the chart, everything becomes much clearer.

I don’t know if you remember, but my price target sits around $19 per share, with a mid-single-digit shareholder yield.

Depending on what kind of EBITDA margin Zumiez manages to pull out of the hat, that range runs from about $13.50 on the conservative end to roughly $24.50 on the optimistic side.

By the time Zumiez put these numbers on the table, the stock was already sitting in the $30s, about 18% above even the top end of my price target. So, it's not hard to see why, even with a double-beat, the market walked it back into the low-to-mid $20s.

In fact, it looks like a pretty healthy pullback, especially considering the stock was already a bit stretched back in late November and early December.

That makes a lot more sense to me than pinning the sour mood overseas.

After all, out of the 728 stores they had at the end of the quarter, only 113 were in Europe and Australia, accounting for just about 15% of revenue.

But like I said up front, maybe I played it a little too safely, penciling in just about an 8.5% upside in the short to medium term using the midpoint.

If you look at how my 'Hold' actually played out, Zumiez ended up beating Wall Street over the same stretch, delivering a bit over 19%, while the Street only managed a mid-single-digit gain.

Now that all the numbers are out in the open, let’s dig into how Zumiez managed to squeeze out those fatter margins and whether they can pull it off again.

The True Engine of the Margins

This quarter, Zumiez posted $239.1 million in sales, up 7.5% year-over-year, the best print they’ve delivered in six quarters. Nearly all of that came from same-store sales, which grew 7.6%, with double-digit comps in North America more than offsetting a 3.9% decline internationally.

But let’s call it what it is. Most of that didn’t come from traffic.

The trick behind those same-store sales gains is pricing.

Think about it. Sell more at full price, cut back on discounts, lean into private labels, and comps move higher even if traffic doesn’t.

What’s interesting is that this runs against the usual playbook in mass apparel retail and looks a lot closer to the pricing playbook you see in turnarounds like LuxExperience (LUXE) with NET-A-PORTER post-acquisition.

And the heavier push into women’s isn’t an accident either. That category naturally gives you more room to premiumize and tends to be a lot less price-sensitive than the traditional skate and street mix.

You can see it pretty clearly when you look at EPS. Last quarter, management was guiding something close to $0.29, and it ended up printing at $0.55, a beat of ~90%.

Plain and simple, a lot of that runs straight through operating leverage, and you can see it in three buckets:

  1. With fewer markdowns in the mix, gross margin moved up from 35.2% to 37.6%, a clean 240 bp lift year-over-year.
  2. Because most SG&A costs are fixed or semi-fixed, higher sales dilute them. SG&A as a share of revenue fell from 34.1% to 32.6%.
  3. Inventory turns also improved. Inventory fell about 3.5% year-over-year, freeing up working capital and improving EBITDA-to-OCF conversion.

Here’s a peek at how the margins stacked up:

On the qualitative side, private labels help push the brand more toward a premium, youth-lifestyle curator instead of just another skate shop.

You get the usual perks (higher customer lifetime value and fatter contribution margins), while in-house brands like Empyre, Dravus, and A-LAB typically price ~20% to 50% below big names like Adidas (ADDYY) or Quiksilver.

As confidence cools, value-per-dollar matters a lot more.

You can line all of this up with the sharp drop in Gen Z’s confidence, especially toward the end of last year. That’s exactly why the push into cheaper private labels makes so much sense.

Revising Expectations

I wasn’t the only one turning more positive on Zumiez.

Wall Street seemed to start warming up too, especially after management lifted FY 2025 EPS guidance to a range of $0.57–$0.67. Looking at the revisions, the Street now expects FY 2026 EPS to climb by about 86%, hitting $0.80.

The pattern holds for FY 2027 and FY 2028, with estimates at $1.14 and $1.88, respectively.

Unlike EPS, the revenue projection remains virtually the same, as the narrative has clearly shifted towards margin expansion.

When you reverse-engineer these estimates, what they’re really pricing in is a net margin close to 1.5%. That’s an improvement from today, sure, but still a long way from the five-year average of roughly 2.3%.

I think the same logic applies to EBITDA margins. With the historical average around 7.2%, my optimistic call of something close to 5% doesn't sound stretched at all.

$24.50 Still Seems Fair for This Stock?

If you stack Zumiez up against peers like Urban Outfitters (URBN), Abercrombie (ANF), Buckle (BKE), and Guess (GES), these are the following average multiples:

  • P/E: 12.1x
  • EV/EBITDA: 9.1x

Staring at the P/E in isolation can be misleading here. Zumiez is cyclical, and when earnings rebound this fast, the multiple always looks richer than it really is.

So, I’d rather sanity-check it using PEG. At about $25, the stock is trading around 40x earnings based on the midpoint of guidance. If the Street’s ~31% EPS CAGR through FY 2028 actually plays out, you’re still sitting at roughly a 1.3 PEG.

It looks more fair than cheap from where I see it.

Moving on to EBITDA, I'd now like to project margins between 4% and 6% for FY 2026, not FY 2025. This will place our view a bit further into the long term.

Thus, with a consensus of $927 million in revenue, we are targeting $37 to $55.6 million in EBITDA.

Plugging this into our model using the peer-group average multiple and Zumiez's capital structure, we land on a fair value range of roughly $21.80 to $30.20 per share. That's already discounted back to today at about a 9.2% cost of equity, assuming a 0.86 beta and a 4.14% risk-free rate.

Note that in this model, the midpoint would be at $26, with the stock only $1 below that as I write this.

If you want to lean a bit more optimistic, assuming a 7% EBITDA margin for FY 2027, just slightly below the five-year average, gets you to about $66.1 million in EBITDA and a price target close to $32 per share.

On the bullish side, that math gets you about 28% upside, on top of low- to mid-single-digit buybacks each year.

It’s a decent payoff, but it also assumes a lot of things break the right way.

So, I'm really looking at a few different scenarios here, each one pointing to a very different payoff.

The first one, and in my view the most likely, is that margins improve but still remain below the five-year average. In that case, you're probably looking at a trading range somewhere between $25 and $30, with low-to-mid-single-digit annual returns coming from buybacks.

The second scenario, and the most bullish, assumes margins normalize by FY 2027, potentially pushing the stock into the mid-$30s.

Scenario three is the downside/flat case. Margins stay stuck. With private label brands performing as they are, I don’t see a return to last year’s numbers. That pins the stock in the mid-teens to $20.

Looking at the full picture, I’d argue Zumiez still doesn’t offer a truly asymmetrical risk-reward setup right now in most cases.

I reaffirm my 'Hold' rating here.

You could hold the stock and aim for upside to around $35 over the next two years, plus a mid-single-digit shareholder yield. To me, that looks like a fair setup, especially since the downside isn't too big thanks to how well the private label strategy is working.

I’m not seeing any meaningful tailwinds for expanding footprint or sales in this weak economy. Leaning on private labels is exactly the right long-term move.

data.title: HORAN Capital Advisors Winter 2025 Quarterly Investor Letter
data.description:
“The desire to perform all the time is usually a barrier to performing over time.” - Robert Olstein

Artificial Intelligence

Figure 1: Top 10 S&P 500 Performers for the 2025 Calendar Year

The investment theme of 2025 centered around Artificial Intelligence (A.I.) and the stocks participating in this economic segment. Just as we wrote in our Investor Letter a year ago, the group of stocks with much of the focus were the so-called Magnificent 7 stocks. In 2024, we wrote these seven stocks accounted for over 50% of the return for the S&P 500 Index. In 2025, this same group of seven companies accounted for 42% of the S&P 500 Index return. As reported by S&P Dow Jones Indices, the total return for the S&P 500 Index in 2025 equaled 17.88%, and excluding the Mag 7 stocks, the index’s total return equaled 10.36%. The S&P 500 Index is capitalization weighted, which means the larger companies account for more of the weight in calculating the index’s return. Although the Mag 7 accounted for a large part of the index’s 2025 return, not one of the Mag 7 stocks was in the top 10 for performance. Noteworthy, though, is the amount of technology-related stocks that are represented in the top 10 list, as seen in Figure 1 above.

When evaluating the market returns and specifically the S&P 500 Index, it is worth highlighting the concentration of companies in technology-related industries. Of course, the S&P 500 Index includes the Information Technology sector (34.4% of the index), but also the Communication Services sector (10.6% of the index). Included in the Communication Services sector are companies like Alphabet (GOOGL), Meta Platforms (META), and Netflix (NFLX). A number of companies in the Communication Services sector are technology-oriented and are seeking growth through growth in A.I. Over time, history has shown the largest companies in one period or decade tend to lose size status as the economy evolves as seen in Figure 2. With this noted, other market areas and stocks might be attractive return candidates in the coming year.

International Rises to the Top

In reviewing the performance of the equity and bond markets for 2025, the area that rose to the top of performance was international stocks and emerging market bonds. As seen in Figure 3, the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (EEM) returned 33.7% last year. This compares to the SPDR S&P 500 ETF return of 17.6%. Out of the equity indexes shown on the table, the second and third best performing markets were the iShares MSCI All Country World Index ex U.S. (ACWX), which was up 32.3%, followed by the developed market iShares MSCI EAFE ETF (EFA), returning 31.3%. Contributing to the international returns for U.S. investors was the weak U.S. Dollar, which contributed about one-third of the return. The Dollar declined about 10% in 2025.

The weaker U.S. Dollar could persist in 2026 for a whole host of reasons, with one being the U.S. budget deficit requiring more debt issuance and a Federal Reserve that seems to be on a path to continue its lower interest rate policy. After the last Fed meeting, the Fed seemed to take a more dovish stance with monetary policy, which could lead to more Dollar weakness. A weaker U.S. Dollar is a tailwind for U.S. investor returns in foreign markets.

Economic activity outside the U.S. is expanding. Combined Purchasing Manager Indices in countries outside the U.S. are mostly above 50, and this is associated with growing economic activity. Additionally, as seen in Figure 4 and reported by S&P Global, the Flash PMI for new orders reported in mid- December showed “output rising across all four largest developed economies (the “G4”) for an eighth successive month in December.” Noted in S&P’s report is the fact that the U.S. has the weakest new orders PMI.

Figure 2: Evolution of the Top 10 S&P 500 Companies by Market Cap (1985–2025)

Figure 3: Comparative Total Returns for Equity and Fixed Income ETFs

Looking Ahead

In the U.S., large company stocks, specifically the S&P 500 Index, have generated strong returns in the three calendar years beginning in 2023. Some investors are concerned this strength foretells a weaker period ahead, sometimes stated as the market reverting to its mean. However, often it is said strengths begets strength, and since 1926, there have been seven other periods where the total return for the S&P 500 was positive in four or more consecutive calendar years. The longest stretch was the period 1991-1999, which was a string of nine consecutive calendar years the S&P 500 Index generated a positive return. So simply because the market has generated strong returns over the past three years, this does not necessarily indicate a down year will follow.

A part of what can contribute to a positive year is a strengthening economy. In the U.S., the consumer accounts for 70% of economic activity. Recent soft news around the consumer suggests a lower level of confidence by the consumer, as noted by the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index. As Figure 5 shows, the index is near its lowest level historically, with a reading of 52.9. Yet, when evaluating the hard data or the actions of consumers, like retail sales, one arrives at a more positive view of the consumer. One data point is the Johnson Redbook Index, which measures year-over-year same store sales growth of U.S. general merchandise retailers. This measure captures approximately 80% of the retail sales data that is also collected by the U.S. Department of Commerce. As Figure 6 shows, the December year-over-year retail sales growth in the Johnson Redbook report equals 7.6%, near a record high going back to the mid-1990s. In short, although the consumer is expressing less optimism, their actions seem to suggest otherwise.

Figure 4: Global Flash PMI New Orders: G4 Economy Demand Comparison

Figure 5: Long-Term Trends in the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index

The consumer strength was validated in the initial estimate of third quarter real Gross Domestic Product (GDP) reported in late December. In the report, GDP increased at an annual rate of 4.3%. Consumer spending was cited as one reason for the strength. Also highlighted in the GDP report by Piper Sandler, "what was really striking was the 8.2% quarter-over-quarter (QoQ) annual rate increase in nominal GDP, with the GDP price deflator up a strong 3.8% QoQ, lifted by the capex, equipment and intellectual property components (i.e., strong A.I. investment)."

Earnings as an Important Driver

One aspect of the equity market (S&P 500 Index) that has evolved over the past number of years is stocks are trading at higher valuations or price earnings multiples (P/Es).

As 2026 unfolds, earnings growth likely becomes an important driver of a positive returning equity market as seen in Figure 7. At the time of writing, analyst expectations are S&P 500 earnings will be up 15.6% in 2026. Assuming fourth quarter meets expectations, 2025 earnings will be up 13.3% after 2024 earnings were up 12.1%. With strong expected earnings growth in the coming year, stock returns could follow a similar path.

Figure 6: U.S. Retail Sales Growth Trends: Johnson Redbook Index (YoY)

Figure 7: Drivers of S&P 500 Returns: The Shift from Multiples to Earnings (2023–2025)

Conclusion

For the past few years, much of the investment conversation has centered around the group of stocks referred to as the Magnificent 7. Importantly, though, and as noted earlier in this Investor Letter, other market areas have performed better than just the Magnificent 7 and the S&P 500 Index for that matter. Broadly, the international markets have led performance with a large percentage of the outperformance coming from the weaker U.S. Dollar. Signs of further broadening in the market are seen with indexes like the S&P 500 Equal Weighted Index. In December and into the first full week of trading this year, the equal-weighted S&P 500 Index has hit new all-time highs. This is partially a sign that smaller-sized companies in the S&P 500 Index are contributing to the index’s performance.

Additionally, there is the potential for investor disappointment and, in turn, volatility. 2025 was a story about two halves when it came to volatility. The first half of the year had a drawdown in the S&P 500 of more than 20%, with the Magnificent 7 correcting even more. The market would then recover its losses within the following 80 trading days. Within the second half of 2025, markets drifted higher with little to no downside volatility. 2026 may experience a similar whiplash and still could end the year with positive returns. As previously mentioned, the current expectation is that net earnings for the S&P 500 will grow 15.6% in 2026, which is above the 25-year average of 10.5%. A revision of these estimates downward would cause a market correction, not too dissimilar to the correction the market experienced in the first half of 2025.

However, there are several economic themes that would suggest a continuation of the current bull market. As further rate cuts are expected from the Federal Reserve in 2026, which is indicative of a more dovish Fed, this action typically is associated with a slow-growth economy or one coming out of recession. In environments like this, typically cyclical stocks and more value-oriented ones lead in performance. With this noted, 2026 might be a year where rotation into these underperforming high-quality stocks occurs. It is not to say the A.I. trade is over, but entering an environment where broader participation is a factor for investors to be mindful of.

Thank you for your continued confidence and support in HORAN Wealth, and we are always available to answer your questions and discuss our outlook further. Please be sure to visit us for company news, reports, and our blog at https://horanwealth.com/insights.

Respectfully,

HORAN Wealth

data.title: Multifamily Delinquencies Rise Again, Hit New Post-Great Recession High
data.description:
By Ryan McMaken

Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac (also known as “GSEs”) have released their November reports on their mortgage portfolios and mortgage delinquencies. Both Fannie and Freddie report that serious delinquencies in multifamily are rising to multiyear highs.

(These numbers reflect the condition of mortgages in each agency’s portfolio, which are a major part of the overall mortgage market. Nearly half of the multifamily mortgage market are part of GSE portfolios.)

For November, seriously delinquent multifamily mortgages (90+ days delinquent) at Fannie Mae rose to 0.75 percent. That’s up from October’s total of 0.71 percent, and it was up from November 2024’s total of 0.60 percent. Fannie’s delinquency rate has risen quickly since December 2022 when the rate was 0.24 percent. Excluding the COVID panic, Fannie’s delinquency rate is now the highest since 2010, but remains below the Great Recession high of 0.80 percent.

Freddie Mac’s delinquency report, on the other hand, shows delinquencies above the Great Recession peak. During November, Freddie reported multifamily serious delinquency rate was 0.48 percent. That’s unchanged from October 2025 but up from November 2024’s level of 0.41 percent.

Comparing for November of each year, November 2025’s delinquency rate at Fannie exceeds that of November 2011, the previous peak year for delinquencies (ex COVID), when November delinquencies reached 0.72 percent. At Freddie, November 2025’s delinquency rate of .48 percent is the highest in decades and above the previous peak of 0.39 percent.

This trend likely reflects slowing rent growth and waning demand for rentals as employment stagnates and wage growth slows. CNBC reported on Dec 26:

After years of steep increases, renters are finally seeing sustained price relief, a trend that appears to be carrying into early 2026.

In November, the median asking rent across the 50 largest U.S. metro areas was $1,693, down about 1% from a year earlier and marking the 28th consecutive month of year-over-year declines, according to Realtor.com listings data. Nationally, the median rent fell to $1,367, down 1.1% from a year earlier, according to Apartment List’s data.

November is typically the slowest month for rentals, but rents fell more from October to November this year than they did over the same period last year, according to Apartment List.

With new apartment supply still hitting the market, rents are expected to remain lower into 2026.

“Barring a major economic shock, 2026 is shaping up to be one of the more renter-friendly periods we’ve seen in a decade,” says Michelle Griffith, a luxury real estate broker at Douglas Elliman.

The phrase “renter-friendly” is anything but friendly for owners of multifamily rentals. Moreover, landlords must continue to contend with rising prices in services and materials necessary for regular maintenance of multifamily units. In other words, we must consider inflation, so real, inflation-adjusted rent growth is even worse than the nominal declines now reported in a number of metro areas. In Denver metro, for example, the median asking rent in November was down 4.8 percent year over year.

Disclosure: no positions.
