data.title: Celsius Holdings: Still Undervalued After Q3 Recovery
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Celsius Holdings (CELH) continues to be undervalued and provides significant upside with strong growth and benefits from PepsiCo integration. After unexpected termination costs (entirely covered by PepsiCo) appeared during Q3 earnings release, the stock fell 30% in two days, a substantial overreaction to positive news. The stock has since recovered the majority of its losses, rising ≈40% from November lows.

After acquiring competitor Alani Nu in April, the growth potential has improved significantly with a 114% change YoY in Q3 for the new brand. This won't continue at the same rate, but growth is likely to outpace the sector, further reinforced by Alani's integration into PepsiCo distribution (combining it with the rest of the Celsius portfolio).

These synergies should cushion the tariff impact for Celsius, which is expected to grow in Q4. Even with part of the impact in Q3, Celsius managed to improve gross margin YoY from 46% to 51.3%. Guidance for Q4 was vague, only hinting it would be a "noisy" one. Regardless of Q4 results, the long-term prospects are still promising and improved in Q3 by bringing Alani into the PepsiCo system. Although Celsius has mostly recovered from its Q3 drop, the company remains largely undervalued and is deserving of a buy rating.

Overview

Celsius has aggressively begun expanding its energy drink portfolio, acquiring both Alani Nu and Rockstar Energy (U.S. and Canada operations only) within a six-month period. The two brands vary widely, with Alani focused on women's health and Rockstar on energy and adventure. This has boosted Celsius's U.S. market share to over 20%, doubling in two years.

Alani sales have exploded over the past year, generating record sales of $332 million and accounting for ≈46% of sales in Q3, helped by the success of their limited-time offering, Witches Brew. This may carry out a little into Q4, as it is a Halloween-themed drink. Another popular LTO is out now, Winter Wonderland, and although it isn't as popular as Witches Brew, it is still sold out on the official Alani Nu site. The company was acquired by Celsius for $1.8 billion in April and has boosted sales and visibility for the brand. This growth should continue at a strong pace, considering it is still a young brand and there is much to do in the way of expansion. The PepsiCo deal solidifies this, providing a much-needed distribution upgrade to one of the fastest-growing energy drinks in the U.S.

Rockstar is a much smaller contributor, generating $18 million in sales during the one month in the Celsius portfolio. The brand was once a powerhouse, capturing 14% of the U.S. energy drink market in 2008, and has slowly dwindled, declining 9% YoY in Q3. This is a change for Celsius, operating in a market different from its typical health and wellness arena. This could be seen as a wild card, where growth could see a decent uptick if a brand revival is successful. Celsius has historically been a great marketer, and if its success can be repeated in Rockstar's segment, it could generate an could drive incremental sales.

The Celsius flagship brand has also undergone some new initiatives. This includes the launch of its Live Fit Go marketing campaign at the beginning of Q3. They signed notable athletes such as Travis Hunter, Caleb Downs, Bryce Underwood, and Jeremiyah Love, which supported above-category growth in Q3, growing by 13% YoY. This was after starting off slow, with sales declining 3% YoY in Q1 and growing only 3% YoY in Q2. With growth back up for the flagship brand, the prospects are only better.

So far, Celsius has followed through on growth expectations, made strong acquisitions, and begun initiatives to improve margins. This has continued to be undervalued by the market, which overreacted after Q3 results. Although the market has started to realize its Q3 mistake, there is still plenty of long-term opportunity to capture before it's fairly valued.

Pressures and PepsiCo Deal

Investors have been focused on aluminum tariffs, which have a large effect, considering more than 90% of volume is packaged in cans. The current rate is 50% on aluminum imports from the majority of countries, which adds pressure to gross margins. However, even with higher tariff pressure, margins have held strong even as tariff pressure has ramped up.

CFO Jarrod Langhans addressed this, saying, "Even with the tariffs, there's still opportunity to take some of that pricing down." This would be done through the scale and further integration of acquisitions. Alani’s integration has already helped, and they expect the rest of the benefits to be realized by the end of Q1.

Celsius also announced a new PepsiCo distribution agreement, making it the official distributor of all three Celsius brands. This caused some surprises in Q3 results when heavy termination costs for former Alani Nu distributors totaled nearly $250 million and put stress on earnings. This is hardly concerning, however, since these costs and all termination costs are fully covered by PepsiCo under the long-term agreement.

What was worrying investors was the lack of guidance for Q4 revolving around unknown scrap and returns, which is a situation similar to 2022 when Celsius faced operational and transitional headwinds during their initial PepsiCo transition. This put intense pressure on margins and will have a similar effect in Q4. Management expects this to disappear during the first half of 2026 once the transition is complete, scrap and returns normalize, and cost efficiencies are fully realized. In the end, this is all just short-term noise, and Celsius will be much better off once it is finished.

Adding Alani Nu to PepsiCo helps Celsius in a way that hasn't been fully realized by the market and has the potential to send this stock well beyond 2025 highs. Integration increases availability, which has historically been a problem for Alani Nu in certain regions, as distributors struggle to keep up with demand. Of course, you also save on margin through shared trucking, route utilization, and warehousing. Shelf positioning will improve, allowing the brand to get more visibility. Outside of financial improvements, it will enable Celsius to focus less on managing multiple distributions and focus on innovation and expansion. The upsides are numerous, and the market has not taken this fully into account.

International Expansion

Another growth driver is international expansion, and although a huge breakthrough isn't necessary, it would be beneficial. International sales grew 24% YoY from momentum in the UK, Ireland, France, New Zealand, and Benelux, which are their current markets. Currently, this makes up 3% of revenue, but the growth potential is something to consider. Monster Energy, an American-based energy drink manufacturer, generated 43% of its sales outside the United States. That international revenue is largely concentrated in the EU, where Monster and Red Bull rule the market.

Celsius and Alani Nu represent health and wellness, with sugar-free and vitamin-infused drinks, as opposed to Monster and Red Bull targeting extreme sports and nightlife. This opens up an opportunity in the market for a health and wellness brand as they continue to develop markets. Although Alani Nu is not currently sold internationally, when the time comes, the integration will make this transition relatively seamless.

Insider Buying and Share Repurchases

Just a few days after the release of Q3 earnings and a 30% drawdown in share price, Celsius announced authorization for $300 million in share repurchases. This was a clear signal that Celsius believed the market was wrong in its reaction to Q3 results and that its future looks brighter than it did before. They have the cash reserves to do this, which is a about $800 million at the end of Q3. This was the first announcement of a buyback program in the company's history; however, it isn't the only positive signal.

Insider also bought shares in the weeks following Q3 results. This included purchases from director Hal Kravitz and COO Eric Hanson. These are the first insider purchases in over 4 years, making this another sign of confidence in Celsius' future. Overall, these developments reinforce the point that the market's reaction after Q3 was unwarranted and that Celsius is in a stronger position than ever.

Valuation and Outlook

Since termination fees skewed GAAP net income last quarter, normalized P/E is the more useful metric. At the time of writing, Celsius trades at 43x TTM normalized P/E. Considering the growth of its two largest brands, Celsius and Alani Nu, whose sales are up 13% and 114% YoY, Celsius could justify a higher multiple to capture growth potential and efficiency improvements from synergies across the business. Although gross margin improvement is likely to slow down or stay flat, continued growth is expected to be strong and can help offset this. A few factors, such as international expansion (Alani Nu) or a Rockstar brand revival, could increase this more than expected.

Operating expenses increased, growing 64% YoY, as they look to invest in marketing initiatives such as Live Fit Go. This is a long-term investment, as they look to secure more market share, an effort that has so far been successful with 13% U.S. scanner growth YoY, putting them ahead of category growth.

All in all, Celsius is expected to grow its revenues 30%, to $3.22 billion, for FY 2026. I believe this is an underestimation, with my expectations closer to 40% driven by a successful Live Fit Go campaign for the flagship Celsius brand and improved Alani Nu distribution. This includes better availability, something Alani has struggled with regionally, and more visibility overall. International expansion could further this growth, although the date for that is not confirmed. In conclusion, I believe Celsius could demand a P/E multiple of 51x, using expected earnings of $1.47 per share for FY 2026. This gives a share price of $75 and upside of 42% from current levels. As a base case, that is my 12-month target.

Monster Comparison

Monster (MNST) trades around 41x normalized P/E. That’s similar to Celsius’s 43x, despite Monster’s expected revenue growth being much lower. Monster is expected to have overall revenue growth of 9% YoY, while Celsius is expected to grow 32% YoY.

Even with Monster's position as a more established brand, the similarity in multiple is unexpected. Celsius is in a position to grow and realize synergies from across its business, paving the way for strong free cash flow. This leaves two options: either Celsius is undervalued by a larger margin than previously expected, or Monster is overvalued. In my view, both are true.

Risks

Tariffs are a risk, and the situation could change at any time. If legal challenges reduce tariffs, some pressure could disappear and reinvigorate margin improvement. However, additional tariffs could be applied in an escalated trade war, which could hurt margins and the thesis.

Growth could slow, harming margins and sales, and could weaken the thesis. However, I see this as unlikely considering strong marketing initiatives and further PepsiCo integration. So far, marketing progress has been shown through above-category growth from U.S. scanner data in Q3. PepsiCo integration should also improve availability where previous distributors struggled and create savings through synergies with other brands.

If integration faces headwinds or delays, margins and sales could face longer-term pressure from transition challenges. Considering PepsiCo is already distributing the majority of Celsius's brands, this transition is expected to be smooth and expected to capture the majority of the benefits by Q1 2026.

Conclusion

Even after a recovery in share price after Q3, Celsius remains undervalued. Growth has been strong, and although not expected to continue at the same rate, it is expected to remain elevated above competition. The market also underestimated the impact of PepsiCo integration. For a brand with poor availability in some areas, this is a huge boost for sales. Outside of sales, synergies are realized by integrating Alani with its broader logistics network. Signals of confidence have been shown through the company's first share buyback program and first insider purchases in over 4 years. All of these elements set Celsius up for a strong 2026 and a real chance of reaching my base case price target of $75.

data.title: Circle Internet Group: Buy As USDC Market Share Heats Up
data.description:
With the overall stock market continuing to trend near all-time highs, it's becoming more important for investors to deploy single-stock selection in order to pick up shares of more reasonably valued stocks that are more buffered in the case of market downside.

Surprisingly, the crypto sector has been a major laggard in 2025, with Bitcoin prices colliding, and the weakness has spread to all blockchain-related stocks, even those that aren't directly exposed to price weakness, like Circle (CRCL). Circle, the issuer behind USDC, briefly touched the high $200s immediately after its IPO last year but has since returned close to where it was trading immediately after its IPO. In my view, the stock's sharp correction over the past few months presents a great buying opportunity.

I last wrote a buy article on Circle in September, when the stock was trading near $120. While I acknowledge that my buy call was poorly timed, I think Circle has become even more attractive from a valuation perspective after its share price declines, especially as USDC circulation and Circle's own revenue growth continue on an upward glidepath. I think Circle is subject to excessive pessimism over the specter of interest rate declines and the recent crypto market cap pullback.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to this stock, here is my full bull case for Circle:

  • USDC continues to gain in circulation. As more and more stablecoin use cases and applications are built out, Circle continues to benefit from a surge of interest in owning and holding USDC, especially as an interest-bearing alternative to holding cash. Currently, circulation is growing by more than 2x y/y. Yes, it's true that Circle's primary means of revenue is from interest rate spreads, just like a bank (the difference between what it earns on short-term U.S. treasury bills and the interest rate it pays out to customers). That said, growing circulation should also help to blunt the impact of lower rates.
  • Top-tier partners are helping Circle gain market share. Circle's USDC is the second-largest U.S. dollar-backed stablecoin, behind USDT. That said, USDC's share is gaining, and this momentum is fed especially by the company's growing base of top-tier partners like Coinbase and Klarna. Coinbase, in particular, just released stock trading, which is another use for funds held in USDC on the platform.
  • Highly efficient business with healthy profitability. The company is achieving 70%+ adjusted EBITDA margins (against its revenue, less distribution costs or interest paid to partners), despite its relatively early scale and huge growth rates.

Stay long here: in my view, Circle has plenty of catalysts to spark a rebound.

Q3 Download

Let's now go through Circle's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Circle's total, unadjusted revenue grew 66% y/y to $739.8 million, well ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $699.6 million (+57% y/y). The wide beat spread is just one indication of how quickly USDC is gaining adoption and share. Meanwhile, revenue after distribution costs (the interest that Circle pays out to wallet partners like Coinbase, who in turn offer USDC yield for their clients) grew 55% y/y to $292 million.

The chart below, meanwhile, showcases the recent trends of USDC circulation. Overall circulation grew at a stunning 108% y/y pace to $73.7 billion. This not only added ~$12 billion in net-new circulation since Q2 (after roughly flat performance in that quarter) but also accelerated broadly versus 38% y/y growth in Q3. It's clear here that Circle and USDC are gaining prominence and trust after Circle's IPO (positioning it in contrast to Tether/USDT, whose sponsoring parent company isn't U.S.-based and is less regulated). Also, partner distribution networks are key to Circle's success, as the share of USDC held on Coinbase grew to 24%, up from 21% in Q2.

And as can be seen in the chart below, USDC's circulation growth of 108% y/y in Q3 roughly doubled the 59% y/y growth in overall stablecoin issuance. Meanwhile, USDC's share of stablecoin transactions grew to 40%, up from 36% in Q2 and just 30% in the year-ago quarter.

Yes, it's true that interest rates are trending down; the company's reserve return rates (what it earns on its U.S. treasury holdings) fell to 4.15%, roughly ~20% below 5.11% in the year-ago quarter. But with USDC circulation more than doubling y/y, the broader base of interest-earning assets more than offsets interest rate softness.

Per CEO Jeremy Allaire's remarks on Circle's recent share growth on the Q3 earnings call:

USDC in circulation grew 108% year-over-year to $73.7 billion. This is tremendous growth. And we're very proud that our growth also represents continued market share expansion. Moreover, the amount of onchain transactions using USDC grew 580% year-over-year to $9.6 trillion in Q3, underscoring the inherent and increasing velocity and efficiency of using USDC as a medium of exchange. This increasing velocity of money is a crucial feature of the Internet financial system [...]

And adoption has been expanding across a range of use cases, industries and types of firms. Overall, the stablecoin market has continued to grow strongly, and Circle continues to gain share. On a year-over-year basis, stablecoins in circulation grew 59%. Because we grew faster than the overall market, Circle's share grew to 29% in the third quarter. Based on Visa's published analysis, stablecoin transaction volumes have grown approximately 130% year-over-year with USDC share expanding to 40% in Q3. And as you can see, the dollar stablecoin space remains a market with 2 leading issuers and a number of much smaller players as we continue to sustain our strong position despite increasing competition.

Circle's profit expansion and efficiencies are also healthy. Adjusted EBITDA soared 78% y/y to $166 million, reflecting a 57% margin that improved 7 points versus 35% in the year-ago quarter. The company managed to keep OPEX (at $131 million) growing at just 35% y/y, nearly half the 55% y/y growth in revenue after all distribution costs were paid.

Valuation and Key Takeaways

At current share prices near $85, Circle trades at a market cap of $19.97 billion. Netting off the $2.32 billion of cash, investments, and digital assets not held on behalf of customers on the company's latest balance sheet against $149.1 million of convertible debt gives us an enterprise value of $17.79 billion.

For next year, FY26, Wall Street is expecting Circle to generate $3.30 billion in total revenue, representing 21% y/y growth. That, of course, represents a sharp deceleration from 66% growth this quarter. Of course, reserve return rates in Q3 were at 4.15%. The Fed cut rates by a quarter point at the end of Q3 (on September 17) and again in October. Consensus is currently calling for two more cuts this year, which would effectively bring reserve return rates down to the low 3s for the year. Of course, partners like Coinbase will also adjust their APY accordingly, and Circle should be able to preserve its RLDC (revenue less distribution cost) margins roughly consistently. Again, interest rate declines will be more than offset by the company's CAGR expectations of 40%+ circulation growth, which it well exceeded in Q3.

Assuming RLDC margins of 39% hold in FY26, net revenue less distribution on consensus' $3.3 billion revenue expectations will be ~$1.29 billion. A flat adjusted EBITDA profile of 57% against this revenue profile, meanwhile, indicates ~$735 million in adjusted EBITDA, positioning Circle's valuation at 24.2x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA.

For a company currently growing USDC circulation at more than 2x y/y, with >20% expected revenue growth next year and much faster adjusted EBITDA expansion trends, these are quite reasonable multiples to pay. Stay long here and buckle in for a rebound.
